scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim refers specifically to the Athenian policy of excluding foreigners from the mysteries founded by Eumolpus.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Though Lucian writes in the 2nd century CE, the rule describes a specific, well-known exclusionary practice of the Classical Athenian state.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the granting of Roman citizenship by an Emperor, a prerogative of the Roman state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian is writing about his contemporary world in the 2nd century CE during his floruit.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The dialogue involves a Roman Proconsul and discusses 'traditional' Cynic behavior within the Roman administrative context.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule explicitly concerns the city of Tarsus being relieved of taxation by Augustus through the influence of Athenodorus.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Athenodorus used his influence with Augustus (r. 27 BCE - 14 CE) to secure this exemption; -15 is a representative date for his floruit and influence.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim names Sophocles and describes a trial in Athens involving his son Iophon.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This event took place shortly before the death of Sophocles in 406 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Philemon was a 4th-3rd century BCE Athenian playwright; the rule pertains to slave-master relations in that setting.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the approximate year of Philemon's death, which Lucian is recounting as a historical anecdote.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian is satirizing the social customs (seating at banquets) of the Hellenized elite in the Roman Empire.
